agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to replace development servicing bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to repeal the current Burnaby Subdivision Control and Development Servicing Bylaw and replace it with a new Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would update the rules for how new developments connect to city services like water, sewer, and roads. If approved, it would change the requirements and fees for developers and builders working on subdivisions and new construction projects in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the proposed bylaw details with your development team or lawyer to understand how new rules could affect your project costs and timelines.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.1
PROPOSED NEW BURNABY WORKS AND SERVICES BYLAW 2026
Purpose: To seek Council approval to repeal the Burnaby Subdivision Control and Development Servicing Bylaw and replace it with the proposed Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Mid-Block Connection Policy up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed Mid-Block Connection Policy. If approved, this policy would create new rules for building pathways that cut through large blocks of land, making it easier for people to walk or bike between streets without going around the block. The policy would apply to new developments and major redevelopments in certain areas of the city. This is not yet law — council will decide at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if your property could be affected by new mid-block connection requirements.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
BURNABY MID-BLOCK CONNECTION POLICY
Purpose: To seek Council approval of the proposed Mid-block Connection Policy.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Temporary Use Permit for indoor recreation at 2150 Douglas Road
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a Temporary Use Permit (TUP) for a portion of the upper storey at 2150 Douglas Road. A Temporary Use Permit allows a property to be used for a specific purpose for a limited time — in this case, indoor recreation. This is not a permanent change; the permit, if approved, would only allow the recreation use for a set period. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this permit could affect nearby businesses or property use.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
TUP #26-02 - 2150 DOUGLAS ROAD - PORTION OF UPPER STOREY
Purpose: To seek Council approval for Temporary Use Permit (TUP) #26-02 to permit an indoor recreation use at 2150 Douglas Road.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Temporary Use Permit for social services at 7375 Kingsway
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a Temporary Use Permit (TUP) for 7375 Kingsway. A TUP is a short-term permission that allows a property to be used for something not normally allowed by the current zoning rules. This permit would allow the site to be used as a social service centre, a place for religious gatherings, and a public assembly space. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider and vote on this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit your comments if you are a nearby business owner or resident.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
TUP #26-06 - 7375 KINGSWAY
Purpose: To seek Council approval for Temporary Use Permit (TUP) #26-06 to permit social service centre, religious assembly and public assembly uses at 7375 Kingsway.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Vancouver-SFU cycling route contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for Phase 2 of the Vancouver to SFU Cycling Connection. This proposed project would build a new bike route connecting Vancouver to Simon Fraser University. If approved, the contract would hire a company to design and build the cycling path. This could affect businesses near the route due to construction disruptions and potential changes in customer access. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider the contract award at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date — if your business is near the proposed route, consider contacting Council about construction impacts.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
CA - (CB - 7030) VANCOUVER - SFU CYCLING CONNECTION PHASE 2
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Vancouver to SFU Cycling Connection Phase 2 project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on sewer and waterworks contract renewal
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed contract renewal for sewer and waterworks components. This decision would approve a new agreement with the current supplier to continue providing parts and materials needed to maintain and repair the city's underground sewer and water pipes. If approved, the renewal would ensure the city has a steady supply of these essential components for ongoing infrastructure work. The specific terms, cost, and duration of the proposed contract are detailed in the council agenda. Business owners who work on city infrastructure projects or rely on municipal water and sewer services should be aware that this decision could affect the availability and cost of these materials.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect the supply and cost of sewer and water components for your business.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
CR - SEWER AND WATERWORK COMPONENTS
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Renewal (CR) for Sewer and Waterworks Components.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to adopt capital project program review recommendations
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on adopting recommendations from a review of Burnaby's Capital Project Program. The review, done by The Stewart Group, looks at how the city plans and manages big building and infrastructure projects. If approved, the recommendations could change how the city handles future capital projects — which may affect businesses that work on or rely on these projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.1
COTW - BURNABY CAPITAL PROJECT PROGRAM REVIEW
Purpose: To adopt recommendations provided by The Stewart Group regarding Burnaby Capital Project Program Review.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Festivals Grant Program spring 2026 intake
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on approving the spring 2026 intake recommendations for the Festivals Burnaby Grant Program. If approved, this program would provide funding to local festival organizers in Burnaby. The decision has not been made yet — it is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, prepare your festival grant application for the spring 2026 intake.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.2
ECC - FESTIVALS BURNABY GRANT PROGRAM - SPRING 2026 INTAKE
Purpose: To seek approval for Festivals Burnaby Grant Program, spring intake recommendations.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: $1,000 grant to Tsleil-Waututh Nation Giving Circles
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving a $1,000 grant to the Tsleil-Waututh Nation Community Society to support their Giving Circles program. This is a proposed donation request on the upcoming council agenda — it has not yet been approved or voted on. If approved, the money would go toward community giving initiatives run by the Tsleil-Waututh Nation.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.3
ECC - TSLEL-WAUTUTH NATION COMMUNITY SOCIETY - GIVING CIRCLES DONATION REQUEST
Purpose: To seek approval to provide a grant in the amount of $1,000 to Tsleil-Waututh Nation Community Society to support their Giving Circles.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 32 and 28-storey apartment towers on Silver Avenue, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to forward a rezoning application for two new apartment towers at 6470 and 6508 Silver Avenue. The proposal would allow a 32-storey and a 28-storey building in the Metrotown Downtown Plan area. This is a first step — if Council approves forwarding the application, it will go to a public hearing and further readings before any final decision is made.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to have a say on the proposed towers.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.4
PDC - REZ #25-12 - 6470 AND 6508 SILVER AVENUE - 32-STOREY AND 28-STOREY APARTMENT BUILDINGS (METROTOWN DOWNTOWN PLAN)
Purpose: To seek Council authorization to forward REZ #25-12 to First and Second Reading at a future Council meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Mixed-use high-rise rezoning on Dow Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to forward a rezoning application (REZ #25-23) for a mixed-use high-rise development at 6645, 6659, 6675, 6691, and 6707 Dow Avenue to First and Second Reading. This is an early step in the approval process — if Council authorizes it, the proposal will move to a formal reading stage at a future meeting. The project is part of the Metrotown Downtown Plan and would allow a high-rise building with both residential and commercial uses. No final decision has been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — if the rezoning moves forward, you may want to attend or submit comments.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.5
PDC - REZ #25-23 - 6645, 6659, 6675, 6691 AND 6707 DOW AVENUE - MIXED-USE HIGH-RISE DEVELOPMENT (METROTOWN DOWNTOWN PLAN)
Purpose: To seek Council authorization to forward REZ #25-23 to First and Second Reading at a future Council meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: City to take over management of 6 townhouses at 4878 Brentwood
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a plan for the City to directly manage six non-market townhouses at 4878 Brentwood Drive. The current operator, Metro Vancouver Housing Corporation (MVHC), has chosen not to renew its agreement. If approved, the City would take over tenancy management after the lease ends on June 30, 2026. This is a proposed change — it has not been finalized yet.

key_dates.label: City assumes management of townhouses at 4878 Brentwood — lease expiry

agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.1
4878 BRENTWOOD DRIVE - TRANSITION OF TENANCY MANAGEMENT
Purpose: To inform Council that Metro Vancouver Housing Corporation (MVHC) has elected not to renew its agreement to operate the six City‑owned non‑market townhouses at 4878 Brentwood Drive, and that the City will assume direct management of the properties following lease expiry on June 30, 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider: Building permit activity report for April 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will review a report on building permits issued in Burnaby from April 1 to 30, 2026. This report gives council information on current construction activity in the city. It is for information only — no new rules or fees are being proposed at this time.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.2
SU: BUILDING PERMIT ACTIVITY REPORT NO. 04 FROM APRIL 1 - APRIL 30, 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with information on construction activity as reflected by the building permits that have been issued for the subject period.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 20-storey apartment rezoning at 5035 Beresford St
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would rezone 5035 Beresford Street to allow a 20-storey apartment building with 215 residential units and underground parking. The proposal is consistent with the city's 2050 Official Community Plan (the long-term land-use blueprint). This is only the first reading — the decision is not final, and further public input may be required before approval.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to comment on the proposed rezoning.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14820 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 10, 2026 - REZ #25-11 (5035 Beresford St.)
Purpose: to permit the construction of a 20-storey multiple-family apartment development comprised of 215 residential units above undeground parking. Th proposal is consistent with the 2050 OCP land use designation for the site.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: New Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a new Development Procedures Bylaw (Bylaw 2026) that would change how the city reviews and approves development applications. This proposed bylaw would set out the rules and steps developers and property owners must follow when applying to build or redevelop land in Burnaby. Council will consider giving the bylaw first, second, and third reading at an upcoming meeting — these are the formal steps needed before the bylaw can be adopted and take effect. If approved, the new rules would replace the current development procedures.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — if you plan to submit a development application in Burnaby, the new rules could change your process.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.1
#14824 - Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to implement procedures for review and approval of development applications

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to amend building bylaw for development procedures
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Building Bylaw 2016. This amendment, called Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026, would change the rules and procedures for how development applications are handled in the city. If approved, it could affect how you submit building plans, get permits, and follow city requirements for new construction or renovations. The exact changes are not yet public, but the purpose is to update the development process. Business owners in construction and property development should watch for the council decision.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and review the full amendment details once published — this could change how you apply for building permits.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.2
#14825 - Burnaby Building Bylaw 2016, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Building Bylaw 2016 (development procedures)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to vote on fee changes for development procedures
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change the fees and charges for development procedures in Burnaby. The proposed bylaw, called 'Amendment Bylaw No. 4, 2026', would update the city's Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. If approved, this could affect the cost of permits, applications, or other steps needed for construction or development projects. The decision has not been made yet — this is an upcoming item on the council agenda.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — proposed fee changes could affect your development project costs.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.3
#14826 - Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, Amendment Bylaw No. 4, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw (development procedures)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Housing Agreement for non-market rentals at Newcombe Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would authorize a Housing Agreement for a non-market rental housing development at 7880 and 7885 Newcombe Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and the developer that locks in the rental rates and ensures the units stay affordable for the long term. If approved, this agreement would guarantee that the new rental homes remain non-market (below market price) and available to people with lower incomes. The decision has not been made yet — this is an upcoming item on the council agenda.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time — watch for the council decision if you are a developer or landlord interested in similar agreements.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.4
#14827 - Burnaby Housing Agreement (7880 & 7885 Newcombe Street) Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to authorize the execution of a Housing Agreement for the non-market rental housing development at 7880 & 7885 Newcombe Street

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Housing Agreement for non-market rental at Marine Drive
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would authorize a Housing Agreement for a non-market rental housing development at 6203 Marine Drive and 6292 Byrnepark Drive. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and the developer that locks in the rental rates and ensures the units stay affordable for the long term. If approved, this agreement would create new below-market rental homes in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect rental housing supply and competition in your area.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.5
#14828 - Burnaby Housing Agreement (6203 Marine Drive and 6292 Byrnepark Drive) Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to authorize the execution of a Housing Agreement for the non-market rental housing development at 6203 Marine Drive and 6292 Byrnepark Drive

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 40-storey mixed-use building rezoning at 5978 Wilson Ave
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would rezone 5978 Wilson Avenue. If approved, the change would allow a developer to build a 40-storey mixed-use building. The project would include market and non-market rental apartments, a child care facility, and a small commercial space for neighbourhood businesses. This is a rezoning application — a request to change the official zoning rules for that specific property. The decision has not been made yet; Council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to speak for or against the project before Council votes.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Child Care
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.3.1
#14759 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 12, 2025 - REZ #24-22 (5978 Wilson Ave)
Purpose: to permit the construction of a high-rise residential development with a neighbourhood commercial unit comprised of a 40-storey mixed-use building which includes a child care facility and non-market and market rental residential units

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Master Plan amendment for 6800 Lougheed Hwy in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would update the Burnaby Lake Village Conceptual Master Plan for the property at 6800 Lougheed Highway. The amendment would establish a new site plan with updated building heights and massing (the overall shape and size of buildings) to match the city's new Official Community Plan (OCP 2050) and its new height-based zoning districts (R6 and CM1). It would also expand open space on the site. No actual development or subdivision is being proposed at this time — this is only a plan-level change.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this plan change could affect future development potential on this site.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.3.2
#14803 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 5, 2026 - REZ #25-18 (6800 Lougheed Hwy)
Purpose: to advance a Master Plan (MP) amendment to the Burnaby Lake Village Conceptual Master Plan to establish a revised site plan with updated height and massing in alignment with the recently adopted OCP 2050 and associated height-based zoning districts (R6 and CM1), as well as expanded open space provisions for the site. No development or subdivision is proposed through the subject MP amendment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Master plan amendment for 4567 Lougheed Hwy in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would update the master plan for the Brentwood area at 4567 Lougheed Highway. The amendment would change the site plan to include new rules for building heights and massing (how big and bulky buildings can be). It would also add city policies for non-market housing (affordable housing not sold for profit), child care spaces, and parks. No actual construction or land subdivision is being proposed at this time — this is just a planning change.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect future development opportunities at this site.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.4.1
#14779 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 16, 2025 - REZ #23-15 (Ptn. of 4567 Lougheed Hwy)
Purpose: to advance a master plan amendment to the Brentwood Site Conceptual Master Plan to incorporate City policies related to non-market housing, child care, and parks and open spaces. Specifically, the amendment proposes to establish a revised site plan with updated height and massing considerations in alignment with the height-based development framework, existing Official Community Plan (OCP), and draft OCP 2050 land use designations. No development or subdivision is proposed concurrent with the Master Plan Amendment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 up for final vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on final adoption of the Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would create new rules to protect tenants who are forced to move because their building is being redeveloped (torn down or renovated). If approved, the bylaw could require landlords to give tenants more notice, provide compensation, or offer the right to return to a new unit at a similar rent. Council will consider this at an upcoming meeting — it is not yet law.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you are a landlord or developer affected by redevelopment rules.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.4.2
#14821 - Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish protections for tenants impacted by redevelopment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to strengthen tenant protections
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009. This change is meant to strengthen tenant protection. If approved, the amendment would update how the city enforces rules related to tenants' rights. The exact details of the new protections are not yet public, but the purpose is to give the city more tools to enforce existing tenant protection rules. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments to have your say on this proposed tenant protection bylaw.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.4.3
#14822 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (tenant protection)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby mid-year fee changes up for final adoption
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of mid-year amendments to the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. If approved, this bylaw would update various fees and charges the city collects from businesses and residents. The changes are proposed to take effect after council votes. Business owners should watch for the outcome, as it could affect licence, permit, or service fees they pay to the city.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect your city licence, permit, or service fees.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.4.4
#14823 - Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw (2026 mid-year amendments)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved new Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026, effective July 1
minutes_briefs.summary: Council unanimously approved the new Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026, which sets rules for how developers and property owners pay for city infrastructure like roads, sewers, and water lines. The bylaw takes effect July 1, 2026. Council also approved related fee changes and created a new reserve fund to hold payments made in place of building infrastructure (called 'payment-in-lieu').
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review how the new Works and Services Bylaw affects your development or property project before the July 1, 2026 effective date.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026 takes effect
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw amendments take effect

minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT the proposed Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026, as described in the report titled “Proposed New Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026” dated May 26, 2026, be approved; THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward the proposed Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw 2026, substantially in the form set out in Attachment 1 of the report, effective July 1, 2026; THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward amendments to the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, substantially as set out in Attachment 4 of the report, effective July 1, 2026; and THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a bylaw to establish the Works and Services Payment-In-Lieu Reserve Fund, as described in section 3.5 of the report. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Burnaby's new Mid-Block Connection Policy
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved the new Mid-Block Connection Policy on May 26, 2026. This policy requires new development projects to include pedestrian walkways that cut through the middle of large city blocks. These mid-block connections are designed to make it easier for people to walk between streets without having to go all the way around the block. For business owners, this means that if you are planning a new development or a major renovation, you may need to include a public walkway through your property. The policy applies to larger sites and aims to create a more walkable and connected city.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are planning a new development or major renovation in Burnaby, review the Mid-Block Connection Policy to see if your project must include a public walkway.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Mid-Block Connection Policy takes effect

minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT the proposed Mid-Block Connection Policy, substantially as set out in Attachment 1 of the report titled “Burnaby Mid-Block Connection Policy”, dated May 26, 2026, be approved. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved a Temporary Use Permit for indoor recreation at 2152 Douglas Road
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved Temporary Use Permit #26-02 for a portion of the upper storey at 2152 Douglas Road. This permit allows the space to be used for indoor recreation. A Temporary Use Permit (TUP) is a short-term permission to use a property in a way that is not normally allowed under the current zoning rules. Council also directed the City Solicitor to register a notice of this permit with the Land Title Office, which makes the permit official on the property's legal record.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Temporary Use Permit #26-02 included as Attachment 2 to the report titled “TUP #26-02 – 2152 Douglas Road – Portion of Upper Storey”, dated May 26, 2026, be approved; and THAT the City Solicitor be directed to register notice of TUP #26-02 with the Land Title Office. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Temporary Use Permit for social service centre at 7375 Kingsway
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved Temporary Use Permit #26-06 for a social service centre at 7375 Kingsway. The permit allows Southside Community Church to operate the centre on a temporary basis. Council also directed the City Solicitor to register the permit with the Land Title Office (the official property records). Additionally, Council approved a nominal licence agreement (a low-cost permission) with the church to allow an accessibility-related structure to extend onto Britton Street for the same period as the permit.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No action required from you — this decision is final and does not directly affect your business operations.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Temporary Use Permit #26-06 included as Attachment 2 to the report titled “TUP #26-06 – 7375 Kingsway”, dated May 26, 2026, be approved; THAT the City Solicitor be directed to register notice of TUP #26-06 with the Land Title Office; and THAT the City enter into a nominal licence agreement with Southside Community Church for the purpose of permitting an accessibility-related encroachment on Britton Street for the term of TUP #26-06, as described in Section 3.3 of the report. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $4.08M contract for Vancouver-SFU cycling connection in Burnaby
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved a $4,083,101 contract to Jack Cewe Construction Ltd. for Phase 2 of the Vancouver-SFU Cycling Connection. The contract covers construction of a cycling route that will connect Vancouver to Simon Fraser University. The final payment will be based on the actual services delivered and the unit prices from the tender. This decision is final and the project will move forward.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT a contract award to Jack Cewe Construction Ltd., for an estimated total of $4,083,101 including taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CA – (CB-7030) Vancouver-SFU Cycling Connection Phase 2”, dated May 26, 2026, be approved; and THAT final payment be based on the actual quantities of services delivered and unit prices as tendered. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $825,000 contract renewal to Hanley Agencies for sewer parts
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved an $825,000 contract renewal with Hanley Agencies Ltd. for sewer and waterworks components. The final payment will be based on the actual quantity of parts ordered and the unit prices quoted in the agreement. This decision was made at the May 26, 2026 council meeting.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT a contract renewal to Hanley Agencies Ltd., for an estimated total cost of $825,000 including taxes, as outlined in the Council Report titled “CR – Sewer and Waterworks Components”, dated May 26, 2026, be approved; and THAT final payment will be based on the actual quantity ordered and unit prices quoted. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council adopted 10 recommendations from Burnaby Capital Project Program Review
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved all 10 recommendations from The Stewart Group's review of the city's Capital Project Program. The decision was made at the Open Committee of the Whole meeting on May 12, 2026. These recommendations will change how the city plans and manages its major construction and infrastructure projects. Business owners who work on city contracts or in construction should be aware that project rules and processes may be updated.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review any upcoming or current city contracts to see how the new capital project rules may affect your work.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT the 10 (ten) recommendations provided by The Stewart Group at the Open Committee of the Whole meeting on May 12, 2026, be adopted. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $127,400 in Festivals Burnaby Grant spring intake
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved the disbursement of up to $127,400 in Festivals Burnaby Grant funding for the spring 2026 intake. This money will go to local festivals and community events as outlined in the report. The decision is final and the grants will now be paid out to approved applicants.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT the disbursement of the Festivals Burnaby Grant totaling up to $127,400 for the spring intake, as outlined in Attachment 2 of the report titled “Festivals Burnaby Grant Program - Spring 2026 Intake”, dated May 5, 2026, of the Executive Committee of Council meeting, be approved. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $1,000 grant to Tsleil-Waututh Nation Community Society
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved a $1,000 grant donation to the Tsleil-Waututh Nation Community Society. The money will support their Giving Circles program, which provides community support and cultural activities. This decision was made at the May 5, 2026 Executive Committee meeting and was carried without any opposition.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT a grant donation of $1,000 for distribution to the Tsleil-Waututh Nation Community Society in support of their Giving Circles, as outlined in the report titled “Tsleil-Waututh Nation Community Society – Giving Circles Donation Request”, dated May 5, 2026, of the Executive Committee of Council meeting, be approved. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 32 and 28-storey towers on Silver Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved moving forward with a rezoning bylaw for 6470 and 6508 Silver Avenue. This allows the developer to prepare plans for two new apartment buildings — one 32 storeys and one 28 storeys — in the Metrotown area. Council also voted to abandon an older rezoning bylaw for the same site and authorized a Housing Agreement to secure affordable housing commitments from the developer. The project still needs final approval (called 'Final Adoption') before construction can begin, and several conditions must be met first.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required for business owners — this is a development approval, not a new regulation affecting existing businesses.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT a Rezoning Bylaw for REZ #25-12 be prepared and advanced to First and Second Reading at a future Council meeting; THAT the predecessor Rezoning Bylaw, Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 27, 2023 (Bylaw No. 14600), be abandoned contingent upon the granting by Council of Second Reading of the subject Rezoning Bylaw; THAT a Housing Agreement be authorized according to the terms outlined in Section 3.5 of the report titled “REZ #25-12 – 6470 and 6508 Silver Avenue – 32-Storey and 28-Storey Apartment Buildings (Metrotown Downtown Plan)” dated May 4, 2026, of the Planning and Development Committee meeting, and the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a Housing Agreement Bylaw; and THAT the items listed in Attachment 3 to the report be established as prerequisites to consideration of Final Adoption of the Rezoning Bylaw for REZ #25-12. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for mixed-use high-rise at 6645 Dow Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to advance a rezoning bylaw for a mixed-use high-rise development at 6645, 6659, 6675, 6691, and 6707 Dow Avenue. The decision moves the rezoning application to first and second reading at a future council meeting. Council also authorized a Housing Agreement, which is a legal contract that ensures the developer provides affordable housing units as part of the project. The previous rezoning bylaw for this site (Bylaw No. 14575) will be abandoned once the new bylaw gets second reading. Several conditions must be met before the rezoning can be finally adopted.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT a Rezoning Bylaw for REZ #25-23 be prepared and advanced to First and Second Reading at a future Council meeting; THAT the predecessor Rezoning Bylaw, Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 12, 2023 (Bylaw No. 14575), be abandoned contingent upon the granting by Council of Second Reading of the subject Rezoning Bylaw; THAT a Housing Agreement be authorized according to the terms outlined in Section 3.5 of the report titled “REZ #25-23 – 6645, 6659, 6675, 6691 & 6707 Dow Ave – Mixed-Use High-Rise Development (Metrotown Downtown Plan)” dated May 4, 2026, of the Planning and Development Committee meeting, and the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a Housing Agreement Bylaw; and THAT the items listed in Attachment 3 to the report be established as prerequisites to consideration of Final Adoption of the Rezoning Bylaw for REZ #25-23. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: [NOT REACHED] Council did not vote on taking over management of 6 townhouses at 4878 Brentwood Drive
minutes_briefs.summary: Council was scheduled to consider a proposal for the City to take over direct management of six non-market townhouses at 4878 Brentwood Drive after Metro Vancouver Housing Corporation chose not to renew its operating agreement. However, the item was listed on the agenda but never reached a vote — likely because the meeting ran out of time or the item was not called for discussion. No decision was made, so the City's role in managing these properties remains unresolved.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
9.1
4878 BRENTWOOD DRIVE - TRANSITION OF TENANCY MANAGEMENT
Purpose: To inform Council that Metro Vancouver Housing Corporation (MVHC) has elected not to renew its agreement to operate the six City‑owned non‑market townhouses at 4878 Brentwood Drive, and that the City will assume direct management of the properties following lease expiry on June 30, 2026.

minutes_briefs.title: [INFO ONLY] Council received building permit activity report for April 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Council was presented with a building permit activity report for April 2026. The purpose of the item was simply to provide information on construction activity, not to make a decision or vote. Because the action verb was 'RECEIVE', this was an information-only item, and no vote was needed or taken.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
9.2
SU: BUILDING PERMIT ACTIVITY REPORT NO. 04 FROM APRIL 1 - APRIL 30, 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with information on construction activity as reflected by the building permits that have been issued for the subject period.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved first and second reading of rezoning bylaw for 5035 Beresford St.
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give first and second reading to Bylaw No. 14820, which rezones the property at 5035 Beresford Street. This is an early step in the rezoning process — the bylaw will still need a public hearing and third reading before it is finally adopted. Business owners in the area should watch for the public hearing date if they want to have their say.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date for Bylaw 14820 if you want to provide input on this rezoning.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14820 be now read a first and second time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to approve the new Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 (Bylaw No. 14824). This bylaw sets the rules and steps for how development applications are submitted, reviewed, and decided in Burnaby. The bylaw was given first, second, and third reading at the same meeting, meaning it has been formally adopted by Council. Business owners involved in construction or development projects should be aware that this bylaw now governs the application process.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the new Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 to understand any changes to application steps, timelines, or requirements before starting a new development project in Burnaby.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14824 be now read a first, second and third time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Building Bylaw amendment 2026 for Burnaby
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has unanimously passed an amendment to the Building Bylaw (Bylaw No. 14825). The bylaw was given first, second, and third reading all at once, meaning it has been formally approved and adopted. This decision updates the rules for building construction and permits in Burnaby.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check with Burnaby's Building Department to see if the new bylaw changes your permit requirements or construction plans.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14825 be now read a first, second and third time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 2026 Fees and Charges Bylaw amendment
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give first, second, and third reading to Bylaw No. 14826, which amends the city's Fees and Charges Bylaw for 2026. This means the bylaw has been formally approved at all three required stages and is now adopted. The amendment updates the schedule of fees and charges the city collects for various services and permits. Business owners should check the new fee schedule to see if any fees affecting their operations have changed.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the updated 2026 Fees and Charges Bylaw to identify any changes to permit, licence, or service fees that apply to your business.
minutes_briefs.industries: Liquor, Cannabis, Construction & Development, Food Service, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14826 be now read a first, second and third time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved housing agreement bylaw for Newcombe Street
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give first, second, and third reading to Bylaw No. 14827, a Housing Agreement Bylaw for Newcombe Street. This means the bylaw has been formally approved at the council level and will now move forward to adoption. A housing agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in affordable rental rates or other housing commitments for a set period.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14827 be now read a first, second and third time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Housing Agreement Bylaw for Marine Drive development
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give first, second, and third reading to Bylaw No. 14828, a Housing Agreement Bylaw for a property on Marine Drive. This bylaw is a legal agreement between the city and the property owner that locks in affordable housing requirements for the development. Council's approval means the bylaw has passed all three required readings and is now adopted.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14828 be now read a first, second and third time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved third reading of zoning amendment for 5978 Wilson Ave
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give third and final reading to Bylaw No. 14759, which amends the zoning for the property at 5978 Wilson Avenue. This means the zoning change is now fully approved and will take effect. The bylaw was passed without any opposition.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14759 be now read a third time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved zoning amendment for 6800 Lougheed Hwy development
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has unanimously approved the third reading of Bylaw No. 14803, a zoning amendment for the property at 6800 Lougheed Highway. This decision changes the zoning rules for that specific piece of land, allowing a new development to move forward. The bylaw has now passed its third reading, which is a key step in the approval process before it becomes final.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14803 be now read a third time. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: [NOT REACHED] Council did not vote on Brentwood master plan amendment for 4567 Lougheed Hwy
minutes_briefs.summary: This item was on the agenda for council to consider approving a master plan amendment for 4567 Lougheed Highway in Burnaby. The amendment would update the Brentwood Site Conceptual Master Plan to include new policies for non-market housing, child care, and parks. It also proposed a revised site plan with updated building heights and massing, following the city's Official Community Plan and draft OCP 2050. However, council did not vote on this item. The agenda shows it was a decision item (the action verb was 'approve'), but there is no indication it was deferred or pulled from the consent calendar. This means council likely ran out of time or did not reach this item during the meeting.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.4.1
#14779 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 16, 2025 - REZ #23-15 (Ptn. of 4567 Lougheed Hwy)
Purpose: to advance a master plan amendment to the Brentwood Site Conceptual Master Plan to incorporate City policies related to non-market housing, child care, and parks and open spaces. Specifically, the amendment proposes to establish a revised site plan with updated height and massing considerations in alignment with the height-based development framework, existing Official Community Plan (OCP), and draft OCP 2050 land use designations. No development or subdivision is proposed concurrent with the Master Plan Amendment

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously adopted the Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 (Bylaw No. 14821). This bylaw is now law in Burnaby. It sets new rules to protect renters, including limits on certain types of evictions and requirements for landlords. The bylaw was passed at a council meeting and is now in effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the new Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw to understand how it affects your rental properties and update your lease agreements and practices accordingly.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14821 be now read a final time and adopted. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council adopted Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw amendment 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has unanimously adopted Bylaw No. 14822, which amends the city's Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. This means the rules for how the city issues and enforces tickets for bylaw violations (like parking, noise, or property standards) have been updated. The bylaw is now in effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the updated Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw to understand any changes to ticket processes or penalties that may affect your business.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14822 be now read a final time and adopted. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council approved final adoption of 2026 Fees and Charges Bylaw amendment
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give final adoption to Bylaw No. 14823, which amends the city's Fees and Charges Bylaw for 2026. This means the new or updated fees and charges for various city services, permits, and licences are now officially in effect as of the bylaw's adoption date. Business owners should check the updated fee schedule to see how their specific permit or licence fees may have changed.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the updated 2026 Fees and Charges Bylaw to see how your business's permit, licence, or service fees have changed.
minutes_briefs.industries: Liquor, Cannabis, Construction & Development, Food Service, Hotel & Accommodation, Spa & Wellness, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Grocery & Food Retail, Retail, Healthcare, Finance & Insurance, Technology, Natural Resources, Transportation & Logistics, Agriculture, Labour & Employment, Professional Services
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14823 be now read a final time and adopted. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved final zoning amendment for 4567 Lougheed Hwy
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has given final approval to a zoning amendment for the property at 4567 Lougheed Highway. The bylaw (Bylaw No. 14779) was read a final time and adopted. This means the zoning change is now law and the property's permitted uses have been officially updated. Councillor Dhaliwal opposed the decision.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or are developing 4567 Lougheed Hwy, review the new zoning rules to ensure your plans comply with the now-adopted bylaw.
minutes_briefs.source_body: THAT Bylaw No. 14779 be now read a final time and adopted. CARRIED (Opposed: Councillor Dhaliwal)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 2026 Environmental Award and Star recipients
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved the 2026 Environmental Award and Environmental Star recipients. Awards were given in categories including Community Stewardship, Planning and Development, Youth, Business Stewardship, Communications, and Green Choices. Recipients include Utta Gagel, BCIT Tall Timber Student Housing Project, Craftsman Collision (Burnaby locations), and others. Staff will contact winners and invite them to the June 23, 2026 Council meeting to receive their awards.

key_dates.label: Environmental Award ceremony at Burnaby Council meeting

minutes_briefs.source_body: 1. THAT the following nominees be approved to receive an Environmental Award: • Utta Gagel, in the category of Community Stewardship; • BCIT Tall Timber Student Housing Project, in the category of Planning and Development; • Peterson, Etro Construction, VEMA Deconstruction, and Stephane Laroye Architect Inc., in the category of Planning and Development; and • Jade Lai, in the category of Youth. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY Resolution No. C2026 - 049 2. THAT the following nominees be approved to receive an Environmental Star: • Craftsman Collision (Burnaby Locations), in the category of Business Stewardship; • Down Syndrome Resource Foundation, in the category of Communications; • Justin Judge, in the category of Communications; • Ryan Konesky, in the category of Community Stewardship; • Riley Williams, in the category of Community Stewardship; • BCIT Bike Committee, in the category of Green Choices; • Rosalind Sadowski, in the category of Green Choices; • VEMA Deconstruction, in the category of Planning and Development; • Burnaby Central Secondary School Access Program 1, in the category of Youth; and • Vincent Feng Lui, in the category of Youth. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY Resolution No. C2026 - 050 3. THAT the recommendations of the report titled “2026 Environmental Awards Program Nominees”, dated May 5, 2026, of the Executive Committee of Council meeting, be released at the May 26, 2026, Open Council meeting. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY Resolution No. C2026 - 051 4. THAT staff be authorized to contact recipients to advise them of the awards. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY Resolution No. C2026 - 052 5. THAT the approved nominees be invited to the Open Meeting of Council on June 23, 2026, to receive their award. CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY
